title: Penetrant Types and Personnel Qualification
narrationScript: Walk up to the penetrant bench in any shop and the labels can read like code. Type one, method d, sensitivity level three, form a developer. By the end of this lesson that language will be yours, and you will also know what it takes, on paper and in person, to be the technician trusted to run the process. Start with the dyes, because every commercial penetrant belongs to one of two families. Type one penetrants carry a fluorescent dye. Under ultraviolet light in the band we call UV-A, the dye glows a brilliant yellow green. You inspect in a darkened booth, with the lamp delivering at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the part surface and the white light in the booth held down to about two footcandles. You also give your eyes several minutes to adapt to the dark before you evaluate. The payoff for all that control is sensitivity. A glowing indication on a black field is the most detectable signal this method can produce, which is why critical aerospace work is fluorescent. Type two penetrants carry a deep red dye instead, read against the flat white of the developer in ordinary light, at least one hundred footcandles at the surface. No booth, no lamp, no darkness. That convenience makes visible penetrant the workhorse for field welds, structural steel, and maintenance calls, at some cost in fine flaw sensitivity. Now the second half of the designation, the removal method, and there are four. Method a penetrants are water washable. The emulsifier is blended into the penetrant itself, so a coarse water spray takes the excess straight off. That is fast and economical for big batches and rough surfaces, but the built in emulsifier also makes it the easiest system to over wash. Method b penetrants are post emulsifiable, using a lipophilic, meaning oil based, emulsifier. The penetrant alone will not wash off. After dwell the part is dipped in emulsifier, and only that timed contact makes the surface layer washable. Contact time is critical, and the bath is never agitated. Method c is solvent removable. You wipe the excess off with a clean, dry, lint free cloth, then finish with a cloth lightly dampened with solvent. You never flood the surface, because solvent sprayed onto the part will strip penetrant right out of the flaws. This is the aerosol kit method, made for one weld on a bridge or a single fitting in a plant. Method d is post emulsifiable with a hydrophilic, water based, emulsifier. The part gets a water pre rinse to knock off most of the excess, then a dip in dilute emulsifier, commonly somewhere between five and thirty five percent concentration for immersion and much weaker for spray, then a final rinse. Method d gives the finest control over removal, which is why high sensitivity fluorescent lines in engine overhaul shops run it. Fluorescent penetrants are also graded for sensitivity, from level one half, called ultralow, up through level four, ultrahigh. Higher levels light up finer flaws but produce more background on rough surfaces, so more is not automatically better. The governing specification and your procedure set the level. Developers carry letters of their own. Form a is dry powder. Forms b and c are the water based developers. Forms d and e are the nonaqueous developers in spray cans, generally the most sensitive choice for spot work. Form f covers special types. One more rule before we leave materials. Penetrant, emulsifier, and developer are qualified together as a family, so never mix brands or types without engineering approval. Now the human half. A method this dependent on technique stands or falls on the person running it, so industry formalized how technicians are qualified and certified. Qualification means you hold the documented training, the experience hours, the vision, and the passing examinations. Certification is the written testimony that you are qualified. In the employer based model used across most American industry, a recommended practice called SNT-TC-1A guides each employer in writing its own written practice, the controlling document for that company. Some contracts invoke a companion standard that turns those recommendations into requirements, aerospace work runs under NAS 410, and some countries use central certification under ISO 9712, where a national body examines and certifies technicians directly. Whatever the framework, the ladder looks the same. A trainee works under supervision and makes no accept or reject calls. A level one performs specific tests according to written instructions. A level two sets up, performs, interprets, and evaluates to codes and specifications, and supervises those below. A level three writes the procedures, trains and examines the staff, and answers for the whole program. Earning a level takes formal training hours, supervised experience, written general and specific examinations, a hands on practical, a near vision check at least annually, and periodic verification that you can tell the colors and shades of the method apart. Certification also expires, typically on a three to five year cycle, because skills and eyes both drift. That system is what puts authority behind your signature on an inspection report.
imagePrompt: In a bright NDT training lab, an instructor watches a trainee wipe excess red penetrant from a steel test plate with a folded lint-free cloth, plain aerosol cans and a UV-A lamp arranged on the workbench beside them.

sections.heading: One Family, Two Dyes
sections.body:
Every commercial penetrant belongs to one of two types, defined by its dye.

**Type I - fluorescent.** The dye glows brilliant yellow-green under **UV-A** ultraviolet light. Examination happens in a darkened area: typical procedure values are at least **1,000 microwatts per square centimeter** of UV-A at the part surface, ambient white light held to about **2 footcandles**, and several minutes of eye **dark adaptation** before evaluating. The reward for that control is the highest sensitivity the method offers - a glowing line on a black field is the most detectable signal PT can produce, which is why critical aerospace and engine hardware is inspected fluorescent.

**Type II - visible.** A deep red dye read against the flat white developer coat under ordinary white light - at least **100 footcandles** at the surface. No booth, no UV lamp, no dark adaptation. That freedom makes visible penetrant the workhorse for field welds, structural steel, and maintenance work, at some cost in fine-flaw sensitivity.

The choice is rarely yours on the day: the governing specification and your written procedure designate the type, and the lighting requirements that come with it are conditions for a valid test, not suggestions.

sections.caption: Commercial penetrant systems are classified by type (dye), removal method, fluorescent sensitivity level, and developer form - and qualified together as a family.

sections.heading: Four Ways to Take It Back Off
sections.body:
The second half of a penetrant designation is its removal **method** - how the surface excess comes off without stripping flaws.

**Method A - water-washable.** The emulsifier is blended into the penetrant, so a coarse water spray removes the excess directly. Fast and economical for large batches and rough surfaces - and the easiest system to **over-wash**, because the whole film, including what sits in flaws, is water-vulnerable.

**Method B - post-emulsifiable, lipophilic.** The penetrant alone is not water-washable. After dwell, the part is immersed in an oil-based emulsifier; only that timed contact renders the surface layer rinsable. **Contact time is critical** and the emulsifier is not agitated, since emulsification must proceed inward from the surface, stopping before it reaches flaw-trapped penetrant.

**Method C - solvent-removable.** Wipe the excess with a clean, dry, lint-free cloth, then finish with a cloth **lightly dampened** with solvent. **Never flood the surface** - sprayed solvent wicks into flaws and strips them empty. This is the aerosol-kit method for one weld on a bridge or a single fitting in a plant.

**Method D - post-emulsifiable, hydrophilic.** A water **pre-rinse** removes most excess, then a dilute water-based emulsifier - commonly about **5-35 percent** concentration for immersion, far weaker for spray - followed by a final rinse. Method D offers the finest removal control and rules high-sensitivity production lines.

sections.caption: The four removal methods - typical use and the discipline each demands.
headers: Use it for / watch out for
rows: Coarse water spray directly on the penetrant (emulsifier built in)
Large batches, rough surfaces; the easiest system to over-wash
Timed immersion in oil-based emulsifier (no agitation), then rinse
High sensitivity on smoother parts; emulsifier contact time is critical
Dry wipe, then a solvent-dampened cloth - never flood the surface
Field and spot checks from aerosol kits; flooding strips flaws
Water pre-rinse, dilute hydrophilic emulsifier dip or spray, final rinse
High-sensitivity production lines; best removal control of the four

sections.heading: Sensitivity Levels and Developer Forms
sections.body:
Fluorescent (Type I) penetrants are graded into **sensitivity levels**: **½ (ultralow), 1 (low), 2 (medium), 3 (high), and 4 (ultrahigh)**. Higher levels reveal finer discontinuities - but they also hold more dye in surface roughness, so background noise climbs with sensitivity. A level 4 penetrant on a rough casting can bury real indications in glow; a level ½ on a polished turbine disk can miss the cracks that matter. More is not automatically better: the specification and procedure select the level for the part, typically verified against qualified product listings maintained under aerospace material specifications. Visible (Type II) penetrants are not graded into these levels.

Developers are classified by **form**: **a** - dry powder; **b** and **c** - aqueous (water-soluble and water-suspendible); **d** and **e** - nonaqueous wet developers, solvent-carried in spray cans and generally the most sensitive choice in common spot use; **f** - special applications. You will meet the practical trade-offs among them in the materials module; for now, learn the letters - procedures and purchase orders speak this language.

sections.title: Qualified as a family - and the procedure governs
sections.body: Penetrant, emulsifier or remover, and developer are qualified **together** as a system. Mixing brands or types without engineering approval voids that qualification and can quietly kill sensitivity. The numbers in this lesson - lighting levels, emulsifier concentrations, dwell windows - are **typical procedure values**: your written procedure and its governing specification (practices such as ASTM E1417, ASTM E165, and ASME Section V, Article 6) always control.

sections.heading: Becoming Certified: the Framework
sections.body:
PT is an operator-dependent method - the same materials in different hands give different results - so industry formalized **who** may run it.

Two words anchor everything. **Qualification** is the demonstrated combination of training, experience, vision, and examination results that shows a person can do the job. **Certification** is the written testimony that the person is qualified.

In the employer-based model that dominates American industry, **SNT-TC-1A** - a *recommended practice* - guides each employer in writing its own **written practice**, the controlling document for that company's program. **ASNT CP-189** is a companion *standard* that sets minimum requirements where contracts invoke it. Aerospace work runs under **NAS 410**, with its own mandatory minimums. Several countries instead use **central certification** under **ISO 9712**, where a national certifying body - not the employer - examines and certifies.

The ladder is the same everywhere. A **trainee** works under supervision and makes no accept/reject determinations. A **Level I** performs specific calibrations and tests according to written instructions and records results. A **Level II** sets up, performs, **interprets and evaluates** results against codes and specifications, and supervises trainees and Level Is. A **Level III** establishes procedures and techniques, interprets codes, and is responsible for training and examining the staff.

sections.caption: The PT qualification ladder - what each level is trusted to do, and the requirements every level must maintain.

sections.heading: Exams, Eyes, and Staying Certified
sections.body:
Reaching a level takes documented **formal training hours** and **supervised experience time**, with the amounts set by the governing document and the employer's written practice. Then come the examinations:

- **General** - written, covering the method's principles (this course's territory)
- **Specific** - written, covering the employer's procedures, equipment, and governing codes
- **Practical** - hands-on demonstration on real hardware or flawed specimens, typically including indication detection and documentation

A composite passing grade of about **80 percent** is typical, per the written practice.

Vision requirements are not a formality in a method that ends with an eye reading an indication: a **near-vision acuity** examination at least **annually** - fine print resolved at close working distance, with correction allowed - plus a periodic check that you can **differentiate the colors and shades** the method uses. Photochromic or heavily tinted lenses interfere with fluorescent work and are typically prohibited in the booth.

Certification expires - commonly on a **three-to-five-year** cycle depending on the document and level - and lapses with significant interrupted service. Recertification by examination or documented continuing performance keeps the signature on your inspection reports meaning something.

checkQuestions.question: An engine-overhaul line must find tight fatigue cracks in titanium compressor blades, with full process control at rinse and emulsifier stations. Which system fits best?
options: Type II, Method A, with dry developer
Type II, Method C aerosol kit
Type I, Method D, sensitivity level 3 or 4
Type I, Method A, sensitivity level ½
checkQuestions.explanation: Tight fatigue cracks in critical hardware call for high-sensitivity fluorescent penetrant, and the hydrophilic post-emulsifiable method gives the controlled removal that high sensitivity requires on a production line.

checkQuestions.question: A bridge crew needs to crack-check one repair weld outdoors - no water supply, no power for a UV lamp, broad daylight. Which combination is right?
options: Type II, Method C, with nonaqueous developer from aerosol cans
Type I, Method D, with dry powder developer
Type I, Method A, with aqueous developer
Type II, Method B, with no developer
checkQuestions.explanation: Visible dye needs only white light, solvent-removable wiping needs no rinse station, and the nonaqueous spray developer completes a self-contained aerosol kit - the classic field combination.

checkQuestions.question: Sensitivity levels ½ through 4 apply to which penetrants, and what is the trade-off as the level rises?
options: Both types; higher levels simply wash off more easily
Type I fluorescent only; higher levels reveal finer flaws but build more background on rough surfaces
Type II visible only; higher levels mean a deeper red
Developers, not penetrants; higher levels blot faster
checkQuestions.explanation: The graded sensitivity scale belongs to fluorescent penetrants; climbing it buys finer flaw detection at the price of more background noise, so the specification matches the level to the part.

checkQuestions.question: Under an SNT-TC-1A-based program, who certifies a PT Level II, and on what basis?
options: The employer, per its own written practice, based on documented training, experience, examinations, and vision checks
ASNT headquarters certifies every Level II directly
The penetrant manufacturer issues the certification with the materials
Any Level III may personally certify anyone at any company
checkQuestions.explanation: SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice implemented through each employer's written practice, so certification is the employer's written testimony that its requirements for training, experience, exams, and vision have been met.

checkQuestions.question: Which task belongs to a Level II rather than a Level I?
options: Performing a specific test step by following written instructions
Recording dwell start and stop times on the traveler
Maintaining the penetrant tank level between shifts
Interpreting and evaluating indications for acceptance against the governing code
checkQuestions.explanation: Interpretation and evaluation against codes and specifications is the defining Level II authority; a Level I performs and records specific operations under written instructions.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §1.4 (types of liquid penetrants commercially available)
CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §1.5 (method of personnel qualification)
Classification practice such as AMS 2644 and ASTM E1417 (type, method, sensitivity level, developer form)
SNT-TC-1A and ASNT CP-189 (employer-based qualification and certification context)
NAS 410 and ISO 9712 (aerospace and central certification context)
